name: Nissan GT-R (R35)
engine: 3.8L twin-turbo V6 (VR38DETT), each engine hand-assembled by one Takumi builder
reliability: 8/10 mechanically - the hand-built VR38 is genuinely stout and the R35 is far more dependable than its supercar pace suggests. The catch is running cost, not fragility: the dual-clutch transaxle, tires, and brakes are exotic-car expensive to maintain.
cost_to_own: Insurance: Very High · Maintenance is the real story - specialist service, ~$1,000+ transaxle/fluid jobs, tires often $1,500+ a set. Budget like an exotic, not a sports coupe.
popular_mods: ECU tune (e.g. Cobb) - large, safe gains on the stock turbos
Downpipes and exhaust to free up the VR38
Upgraded turbos for 700-1,000+ whp on built cars
Transmission/clutch upgrades to hold the added torque
E85 and upgraded fueling for big-power builds

generations.R32 Skyline GT-R (1989-1994): Twin-turbo RB26DETT, AWD - 'Godzilla,' the car that built the legend in touring-car racing.
generations.R33 Skyline GT-R (1995-1998): Refined, heavier evolution of the RB26 formula.
generations.R34 Skyline GT-R (1999-2002): The icon: RB26 inline-six, AWD, import-legend status.
generations.R35 GT-R (2007-present): This car. Dropped the Skyline name. Hand-built VR38DETT 3.8L twin-turbo V6 (480->565 hp over the years; NISMO 600 hp), dual-clutch transaxle, AWD. The everyday giant-killer.

common_issues: Dual-clutch transaxle wear on hard-launched or early (2009-2010) cars
Tire, brake, and consumable costs are very high
Bellhousing/clutch concerns on abused launch-control cars
Generally few inherent faults when serviced on schedule - cost, not reliability, is the risk
maintenance_tips: Service the dual-clutch transaxle fluid on schedule - skipping it is how transmissions die
Use the specified 0W-40 and keep oil intervals short with any track use
Budget for tires and brakes like an exotic
Go easy on launch control on early cars; later boxes are tougher
Use a GT-R specialist who knows the VR38 and GR6
